questions.q: What is the Hindi name for the Legislative Assembly of a state?
questions.explanation: The Legislative Assembly of a state is known as the Vidhan Sabha.

questions.q: How are the members of a Legislative Assembly chosen?
options: Directly elected by the people of the state
questions.explanation: Members of the Legislative Assembly are directly elected by the people of the state.

questions.q: The upper house found in some states is called the what?
questions.explanation: Some states also have a Legislative Council, or Vidhan Parishad, as an upper house.

questions.q: A member of a state Legislative Assembly is commonly abbreviated as what?
questions.explanation: A member of a Legislative Assembly is called an MLA.

questions.q: The main function of a Legislative Assembly is to do what for the state?
questions.explanation: The Legislative Assembly is the lawmaking body of the state.

questions.q: Who presides over the sittings of a Legislative Assembly?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly presides over its sittings.

questions.q: What is the normal term of a Legislative Assembly?
questions.explanation: The normal term of a Legislative Assembly is five years.

questions.q: The Chief Minister of a state is usually the leader of the majority in which body?
questions.explanation: The Chief Minister is usually the leader of the majority party in the Legislative Assembly.

questions.q: A state legislature that has only the Legislative Assembly and no second chamber is called what?
questions.explanation: A state legislature with only one house, the Assembly, is described as unicameral.

questions.q: The state Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to which body?
questions.explanation: The state Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly.

questions.q: According to the Constitution, what is the usual maximum strength of a state Legislative Assembly?
questions.explanation: The Constitution generally fixes the maximum strength of a Legislative Assembly at 500 members (with some special exceptions).

questions.q: According to the Constitution, what is the usual minimum strength of a state Legislative Assembly?
questions.explanation: The Constitution generally sets the minimum strength of a Legislative Assembly at 60 members, with some states having a lower number by special provision.

questions.q: A Money Bill in a state with a Legislative Council can be introduced only in which house?
questions.explanation: A Money Bill can be introduced only in the Legislative Assembly, not the Legislative Council.

questions.q: The Legislative Assembly may make laws on subjects in which lists of the Seventh Schedule?
options: The State List and the Concurrent List
Only the Union and Concurrent Lists
questions.explanation: State legislatures can make laws on subjects in the State List and the Concurrent List.

questions.q: Who has the power to dissolve a Legislative Assembly?
options: The President directly in all cases
questions.explanation: The Governor has the power to dissolve the Legislative Assembly, normally on the advice of the Council of Ministers.

questions.q: The minimum age to be eligible for membership of a Legislative Assembly is what?
questions.explanation: A person must be at least 25 years of age to become a member of a Legislative Assembly.

questions.q: Provisions for state legislatures are mainly contained in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Part VI of the Constitution deals with the states, including their legislatures.

questions.q: A Legislative Council, where it exists, is a permanent body whose members typically retire in what manner?
options: All at once every five years
questions.explanation: Like the Rajya Sabha, the Legislative Council is a permanent body with about one-third of its members retiring every two years.

questions.q: A state can create or abolish its Legislative Council through a resolution of the Assembly followed by action of whom?
questions.explanation: A Legislative Council may be created or abolished by Parliament by law on the basis of a resolution passed by the state Assembly.

questions.q: Who presides over the Legislative Council, where one exists?
questions.explanation: The Legislative Council is presided over by its Chairman.

questions.q: A state legislature that has both the Assembly and the Council is described as what?
questions.explanation: A state legislature with two houses, the Assembly and the Council, is described as bicameral.

questions.q: The State Legislature, as a whole, consists of the Governor together with which house or houses?
options: The Legislative Assembly, and the Council where it exists
questions.explanation: The State Legislature comprises the Governor and the Legislative Assembly, plus the Legislative Council in states that have one.

questions.q: Which Articles of the Constitution provide for the constitution of state legislatures?
questions.explanation: Articles 168 to 212 of the Constitution deal with the state legislatures.

questions.q: The strength of a Legislative Council cannot exceed what fraction of the membership of the Legislative Assembly of that state?
questions.explanation: The total number of members in a Legislative Council must not exceed one-third of the membership of the Assembly.

questions.q: The minimum strength of a Legislative Council, where one exists, is fixed by the Constitution at what number?
questions.explanation: The Constitution provides that a Legislative Council shall not have fewer than 40 members.

questions.q: The Governor may nominate a member of the Legislative Assembly from which community, by a provision that has been the subject of constitutional amendment over time?
options: The Anglo-Indian community (a provision since lapsed)
questions.explanation: Earlier, the Governor could nominate an Anglo-Indian member to the Assembly; this provision lapsed after the 104th Amendment.

questions.q: Members of a Legislative Council are chosen partly by election by local bodies, graduates, teachers, the Assembly, and partly by what method?
questions.explanation: A portion of Legislative Council members is nominated by the Governor, with others elected by various electorates.

questions.q: When the Council disagrees with a non-money bill passed by the Assembly, the Council's power is essentially limited to causing what?
options: Only a delay, after which the Assembly's will prevails
questions.explanation: The Legislative Council can only delay an ordinary bill for a limited period; the Assembly's view ultimately prevails.

questions.q: The territorial division from which a single member of a Legislative Assembly is elected is called a what?
questions.explanation: Each MLA is elected from a defined territorial unit known as an Assembly constituency.

questions.q: The term of a Legislative Assembly may be extended beyond five years only during which situation, and by law?
questions.explanation: While a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, Parliament may by law extend the term of a Legislative Assembly.
